questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the __init__ method in a Python class?
choices: It creates the class object itself
It initializes instance attributes when a new object is created
It destroys the instance when it goes out of scope
It defines the class namespace
questions.explanation: __init__ is the constructor initializer method. It is called automatically after a new instance is created, allowing you to set up instance attributes. It does not create the instance itself -- that is __new__'s job.

questions.question: What is the difference between __str__ and __repr__ in a class?
choices: __str__ is for developers; __repr__ is for end users
__str__ provides a user-friendly string; __repr__ provides a detailed, unambiguous string often suitable for recreating the object
__repr__ is called by print(); __str__ is called in the interactive prompt
There is no difference; they are aliases for each other
questions.explanation: __str__ is intended to return a readable, user-friendly string and is called by print() and str(). __repr__ is intended to return a detailed, unambiguous representation (ideally one that could recreate the object) and is used in the interactive prompt and as a fallback if __str__ is not defined.

questions.question: To define a class method that receives the class (not the instance) as its first argument, you use the ________ decorator.
questions.explanation: The @classmethod decorator transforms a method so that it receives the class as its first argument (conventionally named cls) instead of an instance. This allows the method to access or modify class-level state.

questions.question: Which statement about @staticmethod is correct?
choices: A static method receives the instance as its first argument
A static method receives the class as its first argument
A static method receives neither the instance nor the class as its first argument
A static method can only be called on the class, never on an instance
questions.explanation: A @staticmethod does not receive an implicit first argument at all -- neither self nor cls. It behaves like a plain function that happens to live inside a class namespace. It can be called on both the class and instances.

questions.question: In Python's multiple inheritance, what does MRO stand for and what does it determine?
choices: Method Resolution Order -- the order in which base classes are searched for a method
Multiple Return Objects -- how many values a method can return
Module Reference Order -- the order in which modules are imported
Method Rewrite Override -- which methods can be overridden
questions.explanation: MRO stands for Method Resolution Order. It defines the linear order in which Python searches base classes when looking up a method or attribute. Python 3 uses the C3 linearization algorithm to compute the MRO.

questions.question: You can inspect the method resolution order of a class MyClass by calling MyClass.________().
questions.explanation: The mro() method returns a list representing the method resolution order for the class. You can also view it via the __mro__ attribute. For example, MyClass.mro() returns [MyClass, ParentA, ParentB, object].

choices: An error is raised
questions.explanation: Python uses the C3 linearization MRO. For class D(B, C), the MRO is D -> B -> C -> A -> object. Since B is checked before C, and B defines greet(), the output is 'B'.
